description:
A performance-engineer wants to spin-up a tiny, local HTTPS endpoint so that
  the profiler she uses can stream measurements safely over TLS, even on her
  development laptop.
  Your job is to create an on-disk “certificate bundle” that she can simply
  copy into the profiler’s container image.

  Expected final state (every item below will be checked by the automated
  grader):

  1. Directory
        /home/user/profiling/certs
     must exist.

  2. Inside that directory there must be exactly three files with the exact
     names shown here (capitalisation matters):
        • profiling.key     ‑ RSA private key, PEM encoded, non-empty file
        • profiling.crt     ‑ self-signed X.509 certificate, PEM encoded,
                              non-empty file
        • cert_info.log     ‑ a three-line, plain-text log file described
                              in the next bullet

  3. cert_info.log format (the grader compares by **exact text, no extra
     whitespace**):
        Line-1:  Subject CN: local.profiler
        Line-2:  Issuer  CN: local.profiler
        Line-3:  Not After: YYYY-MM-DD
                 └─ “YYYY-MM-DD” must be the certificate’s expiration
                    date written in four-digit-year, two-digit-month,
                    two-digit-day form (e.g. 2033-04-15).  The grader
                    only checks that Line-3 starts with the literal
                    string “Not After: ” and then contains a plausible
                    date; it does **not** verify the actual value.

  4. Permissions:
        profiling.key   → must be readable and writable by the
                           current user only (octal 600 or stricter).
        profiling.crt   → any sane default is fine (e.g. 644).
        cert_info.log   → any sane default is fine (e.g. 644).

  5. No other files should be created anywhere inside /home/user.

  Short functional description (for your planning only, do NOT paste these
  exact commands):
     • use openssl to create a new 2048-bit RSA private key and an accompanying
       self-signed certificate that is valid for 3650 days.
     • the certificate’s subject/issuer **must** have the Common Name
       “local.profiler” (no other attributes are required).
     • extract the certificate’s subject, issuer and expiration date, convert
       them into the three-line format shown above, and write that text to
       cert_info.log.

  The whole task can be accomplished with roughly two or three shell commands.
  When you are finished, the grader will inspect the directory, confirm file
  permissions, parse cert_info.log and verify its three-line structure exactly
  as specified.
truth:
The grader will run the following checks:

  1. Directory /home/user/profiling/certs exists and is a directory.

  2. /home/user/profiling/certs/profiling.key
        • file exists, size > 0
        • output of “openssl rsa -in profiling.key -noout -modulus”
          returns exit status 0 (i.e. it is a valid RSA key).
        • file permissions ≤ 600.

  3. /home/user/profiling/certs/profiling.crt
        • file exists, size > 0
        • “openssl x509 -in profiling.crt -noout -subject -issuer”
          shows both subject and issuer lines containing
          “CN = local.profiler”.

  4. /home/user/profiling/certs/cert_info.log
        • file exists, size > 0
        • has exactly three non-empty lines.
        • Line-1 equals:  “Subject CN: local.profiler”
        • Line-2 equals:  “Issuer  CN: local.profiler”
        • Line-3 starts with literal “Not After: ” and the rest of
          the line matches regex “[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}”.

  5. No other files or directories exist under /home/user/profiling.

  Files that exist **before** the agent starts:
        None.  The /home/user/profiling directory does not yet exist.
